key_concepts: Simple imputation replaces missing values with univariate statistics like mean, median, or mode. It is fast but ignores relationships between features.
Model-based imputation uses other features to predict the missing values, preserving multivariate relationships.
Iterative imputation (e.g., MICE) models each feature as a function of others in a round-robin fashion.
KNN imputation finds similar samples and averages their values to fill in the blanks.
Modern deep learning approaches like MIWAE (Missing Data Importance-Weighted Autoencoder) and diffusion models are emerging for complex, high-dimensional datasets.

definitions.definition: A strategy that models each feature with missing values as a function of other features.
Imputing using only the feature's own distribution.

examples: Using median house price to fill missing house prices (simple) vs. training a regression model on location, size, and age to predict the missing house price (iterative/model-based).

relations.builds_on: ch05_s01 (Understanding MCAR vs. MAR helps decide between simple and model-based)

exclusions: Deep mathematical formulations of MICE or diffusion models.
